title: Digital Legacy Orchestrator
tagline: Securely preserve and retrieve someone's digital identity — accounts, messages, and documents — for the right people at the right time.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Encryption fundamentals: what to encrypt, where keys live, and why 'we encrypt it' isn't enough.
levels.theory: A digital legacy system stores the most sensitive data a person will ever generate: account credentials, personal communications, financial records, and explicit instructions for what happens after death. The encryption architecture must be designed from first principles, not added as an afterthought. Key decisions: (1) Encryption at rest — every piece of stored data must be encrypted with a key that is not stored alongside the data. Encrypting a database column and storing the key in the same database provides essentially no protection if the database is compromised. (2) Key hierarchy — a master key encrypted by the user's passphrase (client-side) unlocks a data encryption key (DEK), which encrypts the actual content. This hierarchy means: if the passphrase is compromised, you change the master key without re-encrypting all data; if the server is compromised, no keys are exposed because the master key never reaches the server. (3) Key escrow — if the user forgets their passphrase, they lose access permanently. Some systems use key escrow (a third party holds an encrypted copy of the master key) but this creates a centralised attack target. Hardware security modules (HSMs) or threshold secret sharing (Shamir) are more robust alternatives for recovery. (4) Transport encryption — TLS is table stakes, not encryption. TLS encrypts data in transit; it does not protect data from the server operator, the cloud provider, or law enforcement.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory on the key hierarchy — specifically why the master key must never reach the server.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Access control: who gets what, when, and how you verify identity after the holder is gone.
levels.theory: The hardest problem in digital legacy is not encryption — it's access control after the primary holder is dead. (1) Beneficiary authentication — the person claiming to be 'the nominated beneficiary' must be verified. After death, the primary holder cannot provide a second factor. Beneficiary authentication must be established before death (register beneficiaries, verify their identity, associate a separate credential set) and confirmed after death through a death verification process. (2) Death verification — someone must confirm that the account holder has died before legacy access is granted. Death certificates are verifiable documents. A process that requires an official death certificate, verified by a notary or through a government API, before unlocking legacy access is more robust than 'if they don't log in for 6 months, unlock the legacy.' (3) Time-lock and dormancy — automatic release after inactivity is not a reliable death signal. People go on extended sabbaticals. Prisoners lose device access. Time-lock must be combined with an affirmative death confirmation, not used as a standalone trigger. (4) Partial access tiers — not all beneficiaries get all content. A business partner might get account credentials; a spouse might get personal communications; an executor might get financial records. The access model must support fine-grained sharing.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on beneficiary authentication — specifically what must be established before death, not after.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Durability and integrity: storage redundancy, format longevity, and tamper detection.
levels.theory: A digital legacy system is designed to be accessed decades after the data is deposited. Architectural durability requirements: (1) Format longevity — a PDF from 2024 will still be readable in 2044. A proprietary encrypted binary format using a library that's abandoned in 2028 will not. All stored content must be in open, standardised formats with the decryption specification documented independently of the software. (2) Storage redundancy — single-cloud storage with a single-provider backup is not sufficient for a 20-year durability requirement. Geographically distributed storage across multiple providers with cryptographic integrity verification (Merkle trees or similar) at the chunk level is the minimum viable architecture. (3) Tamper detection — the system must be able to verify that stored data has not been modified since deposit. Content-addressed storage (storing data at an address derived from its hash) and cryptographic commitment schemes provide integrity guarantees. (4) Key rotation — if the encryption algorithm used in 2024 is cryptanalysed by 2030, all data must be re-encrypted with a new algorithm without requiring user action. This requires a key rotation mechanism that can operate on the full data corpus without user involvement during the rotation.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on format longevity — specifically the failure mode of proprietary formats and why open standards are a durability requirement.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Legal and ethical dimensions: jurisdiction, executorship, conflicting beneficiary claims, and data deletion rights.
levels.theory: A digital legacy product operates in a legal and ethical minefield. (1) Jurisdiction — digital assets and their inheritance are not uniformly treated across jurisdictions. In some countries, digital account credentials are explicitly not inheritable (terms of service supersede succession law). In others, there's specific legislation. You cannot provide a service that guarantees 'your beneficiaries will get your accounts' without knowing and accounting for the legal framework in each country of operation. (2) Conflicting claims — the deceased's will may name a different beneficiary than the pre-registered legacy contact. The legal executor of the estate may conflict with the nominated digital beneficiary. Your system will receive conflicting claims and must have a dispute resolution process. (3) Data deletion rights — if the account holder requested data deletion during their lifetime, does that right survive death? Most privacy laws say no — GDPR rights are personal and cannot be exercised post-mortem. But ethical practice may differ from legal minimum. (4) Consent of third parties — stored communications contain content from third parties (email threads, messages) who did not consent to storage or beneficiary access. Their privacy interests are a legitimate competing claim against the deceased's beneficiary's access rights.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory on conflicting claims — specifically the difference between the digital beneficiary and the legal executor of the estate.

tasks.scenario: Your digital legacy system encrypts stored data with AES-256. The encryption key is stored in an environment variable on the same server as the database. A server compromise exposes both the encrypted data and the environment variable. What's the fundamental architecture failure?
options: The algorithm choice is the problem here — AES-256 isn't strong enough for this use case, and upgrading to a longer key length like AES-512 would resolve the security gap on its own
A key stored beside the data it protects provides no extra protection if both are exposed in one breach — the key needs separate storage, ideally client-side or in a dedicated KMS with access logging

The server simply needs better general security hardening — firewall rules, patching, and intrusion detection improvements would meaningfully reduce the risk created by this particular key storage setup
Encrypt the environment variable itself before storing it — wrapping the key in an additional layer of encryption on the same server addresses the core issue with this storage arrangement
hints: Think about what 'encryption' actually protects against and what co-location of key and data removes. Re-read the Junior theory on key hierarchy.

Encrypting data with a key stored next to it is similar to locking a safe and leaving the key on top of it. It protects against casual browsing, not against a motivated attacker who gets access to the server.
tasks.why: Encryption protects data when the encrypted payload is separated from the key. If both are exposed in the same compromise (same server, same environment, same database), encryption provides no protection. The correct architecture: client-side key derivation from a user passphrase that never reaches the server; or server-side encryption using a KMS (AWS KMS, Google Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault) where the encryption key is managed in a separate, audited system with access controls that are independent of the application server.

tasks.scenario: This function encrypts data with a hardcoded key. Replace it with a key derived from the user's passphrase using a proper KDF (PBKDF2 or Argon2), and never store the derived key.

checks.label: Derives the key from a user passphrase using a KDF
Uses a salt for the key derivation
Returns encrypted data (not the key)

tasks.why: A hardcoded encryption key means every user's data is encrypted with the same key — compromise the key once, decrypt everything. The correct pattern: derive a unique encryption key per user from their passphrase using PBKDF2 (iterations >= 600,000 per NIST 2024) or Argon2id; use a unique random salt per user stored alongside the encrypted data; derive the key on-demand from the passphrase and use it immediately without storing the derived key. The passphrase should never reach the server — key derivation should happen client-side.

tasks.scenario: Put the steps of a secure data deposit workflow in the correct order, from the user initiating a deposit to the data being stored.
items: Encrypt the DEK with the master key derived from the user's passphrase (client-side)
Store the encrypted DEK and encrypted data (server receives no plaintext keys)
Generate a random Data Encryption Key (DEK) for this deposit
Encrypt the data with the DEK
tasks.why: Authentication before any cryptographic operations. Generate a fresh random DEK for this specific deposit — unique per document. Encrypt the data with the DEK — data encryption. Wrap the DEK with the master key derived from the passphrase client-side — this is the key hierarchy. Only then send to server: the server receives encrypted data and an encrypted DEK, but never a plaintext key. This means a server compromise exposes only ciphertext.

tasks.scenario: Write the data classification policy for a digital legacy system. Define at minimum three sensitivity tiers with examples, and specify what encryption and access controls apply at each tier.
tasks.placeholder:
Tier 1 — Highest sensitivity (examples: account credentials, financial records):
- Encryption: ...
- Access: ...

checks.label: Defines multiple sensitivity tiers
Includes high-sensitivity examples like credentials or financial records
Specifies encryption or access controls per tier

tasks.why: Not all legacy data has the same sensitivity. Account credentials (highest sensitivity — misuse has direct financial consequence), personal communications (high sensitivity — disclosure affects relationships and may have legal implications), public social media archives (lower sensitivity — already public). Each tier warrants different encryption key management, different beneficiary access controls, and different storage retention policies. A flat 'encrypt everything the same way' approach either over-protects low-sensitivity data (expensive) or under-protects high-sensitivity data (dangerous).

tasks.scenario: Your system automatically releases legacy access if the account holder hasn't logged in for 6 months. A user who was serving a prison sentence (no device access for 9 months) returns home to find their account transferred to their nominated beneficiary. What's the design failure?
options: The time limit being used here is simply too short — extending the inactivity window from 6 months to 12 months would be enough to prevent this kind of false positive from happening in future cases
Inactivity isn't a reliable death signal — imprisonment, hospitalisation, and device loss all produce it. Release requires affirmative death confirmation through a verified document, not just absence

The beneficiary should have voluntarily returned access once the situation became clear — the correct fix here is to add a manual reversal process for cases like this rather than changing the trigger itself
The system should send reminder emails to the account holder in the weeks before any scheduled release — prompting them to log in beforehand would have been enough to prevent this specific case
hints: Think about all the reasons a person might not log in for 6 months that don't involve death. Re-read the Senior theory on time-lock and dormancy.
The set of 'people who don't log in for 6 months' includes alive people in many categories. Using inactivity as a death proxy will affect them.
tasks.why: Inactivity-based release will always produce false positives — alive people who lose account access for legitimate reasons. The only reliable death signal is a verified death document. The correct architecture: inactivity triggers escalating outreach (email, nominated contact alert, physical mail if address available); if outreach fails to elicit a response over a defined period, flag for human review; only on submission and verification of a death certificate does the legacy release process begin.

tasks.scenario: This beneficiary access grant function trusts the claimed beneficiary identity without verifying it. Fix it to check that the beneficiary was pre-registered and that their identity has been verified.

checks.label: Checks that the beneficiary's identity has been verified
Checks that death has been confirmed before granting access
Logs the access grant for audit purposes

tasks.why: Two additional checks are required before granting legacy access: (1) the beneficiary was registered and their identity was verified before the account holder's death — not just that their email is in a list; (2) death has been formally confirmed through the system's verification process, not just asserted by the claimant. An audit log of every legacy access grant is also required. 'Email is in the list' is the weakest possible access control for the most sensitive access event in the system.

tasks.scenario: A user registers their spouse and business partner as beneficiaries. The business partner gets access to all account credentials. The spouse gets access to personal communications. Six months after death, the spouse claims they should have access to the business accounts too, which contained significant financial assets. How should your system handle this?
options: Grant the additional access — the spouse, as next of kin, has an inherently stronger legal claim than a business partner does, regardless of what access tiers the account holder originally set up

Deny the additional access outright — the access tiers configured by the account holder while they were alive were deliberate and final, and the system has no legitimate basis to revisit them after death
Escalate to dispute resolution — the system can't unilaterally resolve competing claims. The estate's legal executor and a court order are the correct mechanism, not a system-level access decision

Accept whichever beneficiary's claim was submitted first — using a simple first-in-time rule to resolve competing claims like this one gives the system a clear, consistent, and easy-to-defend tie-breaker
hints: Think about what authority the system has to make legal determinations about estate assets. Re-read the Executive theory on conflicting claims.
A digital legacy platform is not a court and is not qualified to adjudinate competing inheritance claims. These claims need to go through the legal process.
tasks.why: Competing beneficiary claims over estate assets are a legal matter, not a technical matter. The digital legacy system's role is: document both claims with timestamps, preserve the evidence (what access was granted, to whom, what the account holder's stated instructions were), and make that evidence available to the legal process. The executor of the estate has legal authority over financial assets. A court order is the appropriate mechanism for resolving disputed claims. The system should not grant or deny access unilaterally when claims conflict.

tasks.scenario: Write the beneficiary registration flow that a user must complete before their death, so that access can be verified after. Include: what identity information is collected, how it's verified, and how beneficiary access tiers are assigned.
tasks.placeholder:
Beneficiary registration requires the following steps while the account holder is alive:
1. Beneficiary identity...

checks.label: Specifies identity verification of the beneficiary
Describes how access tiers are assigned
States that this must be completed before death

tasks.why: Post-death beneficiary claims are only verifiable if registration happened pre-death. The registration must collect: government-issued ID verification of the beneficiary (not just email); defined access tiers (what content categories this beneficiary can access); a separate credential set for the beneficiary that's independent of the account holder's credentials; and optionally a secondary confirmation mechanism (notarised letter specifying the same beneficiaries, held separately). The harder the registration process, the more trustworthy the claim.

tasks.scenario: Your system stores all legacy data in a proprietary encrypted binary format using version 2.1 of your custom serialisation library. The company pivots in 2028 and shuts down. A beneficiary in 2032 has 50GB of their parent's encrypted data and no way to decrypt or deserialise it. What design principle was violated?
options: This isn't really an engineering failure at all — company shutdowns, bankruptcies, and pivots are unforeseeable business events, and no amount of technical design could reasonably have anticipated this outcome, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes

Format longevity was violated. Proprietary formats tied to company infrastructure leave beneficiaries unable to access data if the company closes, regardless of how well the encryption was implemented

The right fix here would have been to use a larger, more established company's cloud storage platform instead — companies of that size are simply much less likely to shut down or discontinue their services, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under

The proprietary format itself isn't really the issue — as long as it had been thoroughly documented at the time, a future developer could always reimplement a compatible reader for it from that documentation, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes
hints: Think about what happens to a format when the software that reads it ceases to exist. Re-read the Architect theory on format longevity.
Documentation helps but doesn't solve the problem — a beneficiary in 2032 with a data file, a format specification document, and no software to run it still cannot access the data easily.
tasks.why: Format longevity requires: open, standardised container formats (PDF/A for documents, RFC-compliant MIME for email, standard JSON for structured data); encryption using standard algorithms (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) with the specification and parameters embedded in the encrypted object, not in the decryption software; and the full decryption specification published under an open licence independently of the company's survival. If a beneficiary can decrypt the data using only open-source tools and a documented specification, the format has adequate longevity.

tasks.scenario: Your storage layer stores data at a random UUID path with no integrity verification. Fix it to use content-addressed storage and verify integrity on retrieval.

checks.label: Computes a content hash for the stored data
Uses the hash as the storage address
Verifies integrity on retrieval

tasks.why: Content-addressed storage (CAS) derives the storage address from the cryptographic hash of the content. This provides: (1) integrity verification — retrieve the data, compute its hash, compare to the address. Any modification changes the hash and fails the check. (2) Deduplication — identical data stored twice produces the same address. (3) Immutability — the address of stored data cannot be overwritten without detection. For a long-term archive, CAS is the standard integrity primitive. On retrieval, always verify: compute hash of retrieved bytes, compare to the address key. If they don't match, the data is corrupt.

tasks.scenario: Your system was deployed in 2024 using AES-256-CBC for encryption. In 2028, a vulnerability is discovered in CBC mode that allows padding oracle attacks under certain conditions. You need to re-encrypt 500,000 user data objects to AES-256-GCM without user involvement. What architectural property enables this?
options: This kind of migration is fundamentally impossible to carry out without direct user involvement — the original passphrase is required at the point of decryption, so re-encrypting at scale can't happen without it, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under

The key hierarchy enables this: decrypt the DEK with the master key, decrypt with the old DEK, re-encrypt with a new DEK under a new algorithm, re-wrap. The user's passphrase isn't needed for this layer

The correct approach here is simply to re-encrypt everything using whatever key the server currently holds for other purposes — no separate hierarchy of keys is actually needed to carry out a migration like this, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes

Users across the system would need to be asked to re-authenticate as part of this process — requiring everyone to log in again at the same time is a necessary step to enable an algorithm migration of this kind, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under
hints: Think about which layer of the key hierarchy can be operated on server-side without the user's passphrase. Re-read the Architect theory on key rotation.
The whole point of the key hierarchy is that different layers can be rotated independently. The passphrase-derived master key is one layer; the DEK is another.
tasks.why: The key hierarchy enables algorithm migration without user involvement because the DEK layer is under server-side control. The master key is passphrase-derived and client-side; but the DEK is wrapped by the master key and stored server-side. For a server-side algorithm migration: (1) user authenticates (provides passphrase); (2) derive master key; (3) decrypt DEK; (4) decrypt data with old DEK; (5) generate new DEK with new algorithm; (6) re-encrypt data; (7) wrap new DEK with master key; (8) store. This can be done on next login, without requiring a mass passphrase re-entry event. Without this hierarchy, you'd need every user's passphrase simultaneously.

tasks.scenario: Write the disaster recovery plan for a digital legacy system that guarantees 99.99% data durability over 20 years. Specify: storage redundancy, geographic distribution, integrity verification frequency, and the recovery test schedule.
tasks.placeholder:
Data Durability Architecture:
1. Storage redundancy:...
2. Geographic distribution:...
3. Integrity verification:...

checks.label: Specifies storage redundancy
Specifies geographic distribution
Specifies integrity verification frequency
Specifies a recovery test schedule

tasks.why: 99.99% durability over 20 years requires: 3+ copies on different storage infrastructure (at least 2 different cloud providers or providers plus on-premise); geographic distribution across at least 2 regions or countries (not just availability zones, which can share physical infrastructure); cryptographic integrity verification on at least a monthly schedule (detect silent bit rot before it propagates); and annual recovery drills where you restore a sample dataset from backup and verify decryption. Without regular drills, you don't know your recovery actually works until you need it.

tasks.scenario: You want to launch your digital legacy product in the UK, Germany, and India. In which jurisdiction is the legal situation most complex and why?
options: The UK presents the most complex legal situation among the three, given the scale and detail of its overall regulatory compliance requirements for any company handling personal data

Germany presents the most complex legal situation among the three, since the post-Brexit version of GDPR that the UK now operates under is meaningfully stricter than the version still applied within Germany itself
India — digital asset inheritance is poorly defined in Indian succession law, creating real ambiguity about what can legally be promised. UK and German GDPR frameworks have far more legal precedent
All three jurisdictions present an equivalent level of legal complexity here, since GDPR as a framework applies uniformly across all of them regardless of which specific country is being considered
hints: Think about which jurisdiction has the least settled legal framework for digital asset inheritance specifically. Re-read the Executive theory on jurisdiction.

GDPR is challenging but well-understood with clear precedent. The ambiguity in Indian succession law creates a different kind of challenge: you can't clearly promise what beneficiaries are legally entitled to.
tasks.why: German and UK GDPR frameworks are complex but well-understood, with established legal precedent, clear data subject rights, and defined obligations. Indian succession law has not yet definitively settled how digital accounts and credentials are treated under the Indian Succession Act. This creates genuine legal ambiguity: can you legally promise that beneficiaries will inherit access to digital accounts? What if the account's terms of service (governed by US law) say credentials are non-transferable? Launch in Germany and UK with well-understood frameworks first; engage Indian legal counsel with specific expertise in digital assets before launching in India.

tasks.scenario: Write the privacy policy section that addresses what happens to a deceased user's data, specifically covering: data retention after death, third-party communications stored in the legacy vault, GDPR rights that do not survive death, and the process for disputed beneficiary claims.
tasks.placeholder:
What happens to your data after you die:

Data retention:...
Third-party content:...

checks.label: States a retention policy after death
Addresses third-party content in stored communications
States that certain GDPR rights do not survive death
States a process for disputed claims

tasks.why: Privacy policy sections about post-death data must address: (1) specific retention periods (how long data is kept after confirmed death and legacy distribution); (2) third-party content (stored email threads contain communications from people who are still alive and have their own privacy rights); (3) explicitly state which GDPR rights do not survive death (right of access, right to erasure, right to object are personal rights that cannot be exercised post-mortem); (4) disputed claims process (not 'the executor can contact us' but a specific escalation path with timeframes). Vague privacy policies for sensitive data attract regulatory attention.

tasks.scenario: An executor of an estate demands all of the deceased's stored data on the grounds that they have legal authority over the estate. The deceased had specifically excluded this person from their digital legacy vault and nominated a different beneficiary. What's the correct response?
options: Comply with the demand — a legal executor of an estate holds authority over all estate assets by definition, and that authority naturally extends to cover digital account credentials and personal communications as well

Don't comply unilaterally — an executor's authority over financial assets doesn't clearly extend to account credentials or personal communications. A court order specific to digital assets is required

Comply with the demand, but only on the condition that the executor agrees to a confidentiality arrangement governing exactly what they're permitted to access and how that access can be used afterward, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes

Ignore the executor's demand entirely — the account holder's originally registered legacy instructions should always take precedence over any later claim made through the formal legal estate process, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under
hints: Think about what a legal executor's authority actually covers and whether it extends to password access and personal communications. Re-read the Executive theory on conflicting claims.
'Executor of the estate' has specific legal authority over financial assets. Its extension to digital account credentials and personal communications is legally contested in most jurisdictions.
tasks.why: An executor's authority over digital assets varies significantly by jurisdiction and by what 'digital asset' means. Account credentials are not financial instruments. Personal communications may not be estate property at all under many legal frameworks. The correct response to a legal executor demand: acknowledge the demand, note the conflict with the registered legacy instructions, provide the evidence to a legal process, and require a court order specifically addressing digital account and communication access before granting access that conflicts with the account holder's registered instructions. This protects you legally while ensuring the legal process resolves the conflict.

tasks.scenario: Your digital legacy product has been operating for 5 years. You receive a buyout offer from a large company. As part of due diligence, they want to review a sample of stored user data to assess the data quality and content. What is the correct response?
options: Allow the review to go ahead — due diligence of this kind is a completely standard part of any acquisition process, and the acquiring company will be legally bound by confidentiality obligations once they sign on, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes

Decline — data collected for legacy storage can't lawfully be repurposed for acquirer due diligence under GDPR's purpose limitation. Sharing user content this way is outside the original consent scope

Anonymise the data before sharing it for the review — removing names and other directly identifying details from the dataset is generally sufficient to make personal data like this safely shareable for due diligence, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under

Allow access to metadata only as part of the review — details like file sizes and creation dates aren't considered personal data in their own right, so sharing that layer of information alone should be unproblematic, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes
hints: Think about what the data was collected for and whether due diligence falls within that purpose. Re-read the Executive theory on jurisdiction and consent.

Purpose limitation under GDPR is strict: data collected for one purpose cannot be used for a different purpose, even under confidentiality. Providing a potential acquirer access to legacy vault contents is not a purpose the users consented to.
tasks.why: GDPR's purpose limitation principle means data collected for digital legacy storage cannot be used for acquirer due diligence. The data is highly sensitive (account credentials, personal communications) and was provided under a specific trust relationship. Standard due diligence alternatives that don't breach this: share technical architecture documentation, a sample of synthetic data that demonstrates the data structure, aggregated statistics (user count, average data volume, data types), and financial/operational metrics. The acquirer can evaluate the business without accessing user content.
